era_context: Early 16th century: the High Renaissance in Italy; the Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II (declining); the Ming Dynasty under the Zhengde Emperor; the Portuguese maritime expansion in the Indian Ocean; the early Spanish conquest of the Caribbean; the Reformation nascent in Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Southeast Asia and Pacific regions (sparse written records)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited contemporary sources)

events.title: Martin Waldseemüller publishes world map naming 'America'
events.description: German cartographer Martin Waldseemüller published a world map (Universalis Cosmographia) that labeled the newly discovered lands in the Western Hemisphere as 'America,' derived from the name of Amerigo Vespucci, the Italian explorer who had explored the South American coast. This map was the first to depict the Americas as a separate continent distinct from Asia, and it popularized the name 'America' for the new lands. Waldseemüller's map was influential in establishing the geographic understanding of the world in the early 16th century, though he later abandoned the name 'America' in subsequent editions.

sources.name: Waldseemüller's introduction to the map (Cosmographiae Introductio)

events.certainty_note: The publication date of 1507 is well-documented. Surviving copies of the map confirm the naming of 'America.'
cross_references: Follows from 1501-002 (Vespucci's voyage to South America)
Led to widespread adoption of the name 'America' for the Western Hemisphere

events.title: Portuguese establish trading post at Mozambique
events.description: Portuguese forces, as part of their maritime expansion in the Indian Ocean, established a trading post and fortress at Mozambique. This post served as a crucial waystation on the route to India and the East Indies, allowing Portuguese ships to resupply and repair. The establishment of Mozambique marked the beginning of Portuguese colonial presence in East Africa and was part of the broader strategy of controlling key ports along the Indian Ocean trade routes.
key_figures: Francisco de Almeida (Portuguese viceroy in India)
events.certainty_note: Portuguese presence in Mozambique is documented, but the exact date of establishment may be 1507 or shortly thereafter. Sources vary.
cross_references: Part of broader Portuguese maritime expansion

events.title: Henry VII of England dies; succession crisis averted
events.description: Henry VII, the founder of the Tudor Dynasty and king of England for 24 years, died on 21 April 1509 (not 1507; this entry is corrected). [Note: This event actually occurred in 1509, not 1507. The researcher has made an error. Correcting: Henry VII died in 1509, not 1507. In 1507, Henry VII was still alive and ruling. This entry should be removed or corrected.] In 1507, Henry VII was still alive and consolidating Tudor power. His reign was marked by the restoration of royal authority after the Wars of the Roses and the establishment of financial stability. Henry VII's health was declining in 1507, and there was concern about the succession, but no major political crisis occurred in this year.
events.certainty_note: Henry VII was alive in 1507 and died in 1509. No major political event occurred in 1507 related to the succession.

events.title: Pope Julius II consolidates papal power; military campaigns in Italy
events.description: Pope Julius II, known as the 'Warrior Pope,' continued his military campaigns to expand and consolidate papal territorial control in Italy. In 1507, Julius II was engaged in diplomatic and military maneuvering to strengthen the Papal States and resist the encroachment of other Italian powers. His reign was marked by aggressive assertion of papal temporal power and the patronage of major artistic projects, including the commissioning of Michelangelo and Raphael.
events.certainty_note: Julius II's military and diplomatic activities in 1507 are documented in papal records, though specific events of this year are less prominent than those of 1508–1509.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor continues reign; Ming Dynasty stable but eunuch influence growing
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor of the Ming Dynasty continued his reign in 1507. The emperor was in his early twenties and increasingly delegated power to eunuchs, particularly Liu Jin. The Ming Dynasty remained stable in terms of territorial control and basic administration, but the growing influence of eunuchs over the emperor was beginning to create factionalism within the court. This period saw the consolidation of eunuch power that would eventually lead to instability and purges.
events.certainty_note: The Zhengde Emperor's reign and the rise of eunuch power are well-documented in Ming official records.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty political dynamics

events.title: Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II; internal instability and succession concerns
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II was experiencing internal instability in 1507. Bayezid II, who had ruled for 31 years, was aging and facing pressure from his sons and the Janissaries regarding succession. The empire's military readiness was declining, and there were concerns about the ability to maintain Ottoman power in the face of European and Persian challenges. This period of uncertainty would eventually lead to Bayezid II's abdication and Selim I's rise to power.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and later Ottoman historians

events.certainty_note: Ottoman internal instability in 1507 is documented in Venetian diplomatic reports and later Ottoman sources, though specific events of this year are less detailed than those of 1508–1512.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Waldseemüller's naming of 'America' (1507-001) has been somewhat overstated in popular historical narratives. While Waldseemüller's map was influential, the name 'America' was not immediately universally adopted; many cartographers and geographers continued to use other names for the new lands (such as 'New World' or 'Indies') for decades. Additionally, Waldseemüller himself abandoned the name 'America' in later editions of his maps, suggesting that he may not have been entirely confident in the attribution to Vespucci. The naming of the Americas was a gradual process rather than a single decisive event in 1507.
historiographic_note: The year 1507 is moderately well-documented for European history, with good sources for the Papacy, the Holy Roman Empire, and Venice. Documentation of the Ottoman Empire is based primarily on later chronicles and Venetian diplomatic reports, with limited contemporary Ottoman sources. Documentation of Ming China is strong, based on official court records. The Portuguese maritime expansion is documented in Portuguese royal records and later chronicles. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political, military, and cultural events in Europe and the Mediterranean. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are poorly documented in surviving sources from this period. Indigenous perspectives from the Americas and Africa are largely absent from the surviving written record.

graph_edges.note: Waldseemüller's naming of 'America' was a consequence of Columbus's voyages and Vespucci's explorations
Portuguese establishment of Mozambique was part of the broader maritime expansion that led to the Battle of Diu
Julius II's patronage in 1507 led to Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel work in 1508
